nativeRange.habitat: Hill streams to major rivers, irrigation canals, ponds, lakes, and ditches in shallow quiet zones with submerged vegetation or leaf litter; Western Ghats mountain drainages in Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Cryptocoryne, Vallisneria, floating plants)
leaf litter (Indian almond, oak leaves) for natural biotope tannins
open mid-water swimming lanes for schooling and male display

diet.notes: Wild diet includes diatoms, algae, organic detritus, small insects, worms, crustaceans, and zooplankton. In captivity accepts high-quality dried flakes and micro-granules as a staple; supplement regularly with small live or frozen foods (bloodworm, Daphnia, Artemia) to bring out best colour. Vigorous feeder that may outcompete slower or more timid tankmates at feeding time.

avoidWith: very small or slow-moving fish that may be out-competed at feeding
long-finned varieties of the same or other species (minor fin-nipping risk in small groups)

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Irvin Calicut. Wild specimen photographed in Thottumukkam, Kerala, India, September 2015. Shows typical banding pattern in natural habitat context.
Photographer: Nativeplants garden. Photographed June 2024 at ~12°N 75°E (Karnataka/Kerala region). 4080×3060 px.
FishBase species ID 24700. Multiple images including specimen from Karnataka, India (Regional Research Centre, CIFA, 2001, 50 mm). CC-BY-NC applies to at least one image on page.

summary: The Melon Barb is a small, peaceful schooling cyprinid endemic to India's Western Ghats. Males display three bold black vertical bars on a silvery-green body with a distinctive reddish-melon flush on the belly during courtship. An excellent community fish for soft, slightly acidic water; benefits from large, mixed-sex groups of 8–10 or more.
careNotes:
The Melon Barb (Haludaria fasciata) is an endemic Western Ghats species found in streams, rivers, canals, and ponds across Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu in southern India. It has undergone significant taxonomic revision: originally described as Cirrhinus fasciatus (Jerdon, 1849), it passed through Barbus fasciatus and Puntius fasciatus before Pethiyagoda et al. erected the genus Dravidia in 2012; as that name was preoccupied, Haludaria was published as the replacement name in 2013. Not to be confused with the "Melon Discus," a colour form of Symphysodon aequifasciatus from a completely different family.

A minimum group of 8–10 individuals is strongly recommended — males display their most vibrant reddish-melon belly colouration and compete actively when kept in larger, mixed-sex schools. Solitary or small groups show washed-out colour and stress. A 90 cm × 30 cm footprint (approximately 80 L) is the practical minimum for such a group, though a larger tank (120 L+) is preferable to dilute male-to-male competition and allow adequate swimming lanes.

Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 2–10 dH, approximately 36–179 mg/L CaCO₃) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5). Temperature tolerance is 22–26 °C; the species originates from Western Ghats hill streams that can run relatively cool, making it adaptable to unheated or lightly heated setups in temperate climates. Decorate with dense planting, leaf litter for tannins, smooth river pebbles, and driftwood to replicate the natural biotope. Filtration turnover of 4–5× tank volume per hour is advised.

The species is a vigorous feeder and may outcompete slower tankmates; feed in multiple spots or use targeted feeding to ensure all fish receive food. High-quality flakes and micro-granules as a staple, supplemented regularly with small live or frozen foods (bloodworm, Daphnia, Artemia), bring out the best colouration. IUCN status: Least Concern (assessed 2010), though it is a range-restricted endemic.
breedingNotes: An egg-scattering free spawner with no parental care. Conditioning males and females separately on live and frozen foods for 1–2 weeks prior to spawning improves results. A dedicated breeding tank (40–60 L) with a fine-leaved plant mat (Java moss, hornwort) or spawning mop over a mesh bottom prevents egg predation. Slightly warmer, soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 2–4) and a gradual morning temperature increase to ~26°C can trigger spawning. Females scatter adhesive eggs among plants; adults must be removed immediately after spawning as they will consume eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours; fry are free-swimming within 3–4 days. First foods: infusoria or commercial liquid fry food, progressing to micro-worms and finely crushed flake as they grow. Breeding difficulty is moderate due to the need for a separate setup and careful egg management.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scattering free spawner; no parental care. Requires dedicated breeding tank with fine-leaved plants or spawning mop over mesh to protect eggs from parental predation. Adults must be removed immediately post-spawn. Eggs hatch 24–48 hours; fry free-swimming within 3–4 days. First foods: infusoria, then micro-worms and finely crushed flake.
